# Deeprecursion

> Research notes by Markus Fix. Rough consensus and running code.

## Posts

- [The Lab Journal: Writing the Laboratory Notebook, for Agents](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-04-15-lab-journal.md): A plain-text session-logging convention extracted from the Cambrian project, where it produced 46 dated entries across 11 days. Adapts Howard Kanare's *Writing the Laboratory Notebook* (ACS, 1985) to AI-assisted software engineering: permanence (append-only), immediacy (record as you work), self-containment (entries stand alone), completeness (failures and wrong diagnoses stay in the record), witnessing (signed + dated + linked to commits). Why it matters specifically for AI sessions: context compaction erases conversation memory, the writer is often a different agent instance than the reader, and append-only prevents retroactive narrative cleanup. Includes a hypothesis-before-experiment table. Standalone repo at github.com/lispmeister/lab-journal — three files and a paste-in CLAUDE.md block.
- [CAMBRIAN: The Baldwin Effect](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-04-15-cambrian-baldwin.md): Seventeen days after M1. The journey from 50% → 4% → 80% viability. Ten consecutive manifest-stage failures traced to a hardcoded "python src/prime.py" in supervisor/prime_runner.py — not the LLM. A 10-gen campaign hit 8/10 viable. Differential phenotype analysis across the 8 viable organisms found four quality patterns (time.monotonic, exponential backoff, specific exceptions, structlog correctness) that separated top-ranked from bottom-ranked without any of them blocking promotion. scripts/distill_campaign.py automates the Baldwin Effect: AST-based discovery of what the spec should have said, feeding amendments back into the genome. Adversarial hardening: API key stripped from Test Rig, NetworkMode=none, reverse-run regression guard, container log capture before rollback. 305 tests pass. M3 counterfactual baseline battery scaffolded.
- [CAMBRIAN: It Reproduces](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-29-cambrian-reproduces.md): M1 complete. Two spec patches (streaming requirement, aiohttp test pattern) eliminated the whole gen-2 failure class. Gen-1 ran 44 minutes, 10 generations, 5 viable offspring — all at 100% test pass rate. 474,834 cumulative tokens, ~$1.50, 50% first-try success rate. Parse repair worked on gen-4. Informed retry worked every time. Outlook: M2 spec mutation, container isolation, fitness vectors, population campaigns.
- [CAMBRIAN: The Loop Closes](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-27-cambrian-first-loop.md): Nine days after the Phase 1 kickoff: Gen-1 ran its generation loop for the first time. It generated Gen-2 (12 files, ~$0.08), submitted it to the test rig, received a failure report (5 failing tests, deprecated async decorator), and rolled back — all autonomously. The loop mechanics work. Documents what Phase 1 actually required: Python 3.14 SyntaxError constraints, Docker Desktop socket quirks, streaming API requirement, parse_files() state machine fix, test rig vs. organism distinction.
- [CAMBRIAN: What If the Spec Is the Organism?](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-18-cambrian.md): What if instead of patching code, we evolved the specification and regenerated the agent from scratch each generation? Introduces CAMBRIAN — an evolutionary model where specs are genomes, LLMs are compilers, and resource constraints are natural selection. Includes CAMBRIAN-SPEC-001: a 300-line generative specification for Loom, mutation strategy analysis, economic viability assessment, and a phased roadmap from local evolution to distributed spawning.
- [It Rewrote Itself: Loom's First Autonomous Self-Modification](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-17-it-rewrote-itself.md): Gen-72: first autonomous code modification promoted to master. A ClojureScript agent modified its own source, wrote tests, and passed two-stage verification (236 tests + LLM review) in 56 seconds. Documents 17 autonomous generations across 3 models (Haiku, Minimax M2.5, Opus 4.6), six infrastructure bugs found and fixed, multi-provider LLM support, and cost analysis.
- [First Light: Loom's Self-Modification Pipeline in 2,214 Lines](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-16-loom-mvp.md): The MVP field report: 2,214 lines of ClojureScript, 90 tests, 17 generations run. From early failures through five critical bug fixes to a 5/5 stable pipeline. Architecture review findings, generation-by-generation history, the sequence diagram, and what's needed to close the recursive loop.
- [The program.md Protocol: Steering Self-Improvement with a Contract](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-13-program-md.md): How to steer a self-improving agent with a collaboratively written contract. Refined execution model: user and Prime draft program.md, autonomous Lab executes it, Prime verifies independently, git tracks every generation. State continuity across generations identified as the hard problem.
- [The Prime and the Lab: Recursive Self-Improvement for Coding Agents](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-12-recursive-self-improvement.md): A ClojureScript agent running in an Apple container, modifying itself, and testing modifications in a second container before promoting them. Architecture spec for recursive self-improvement using homoiconic code, VM-level isolation, and a keep/revert loop.
- [Loom in Lean: Bootstrapping a Verified Self-Improving Agent](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-11-loom-in-lean.md): Why we moved past Elixir to Lean. The type checker as an incorruptible judge, memory as the beachhead, practical limitations, and an honest assessment of what kills this. **Update:** Lean+QMD memory approach abandoned in favor of ByteRover.
- [The Autoresearch Pattern: A Blueprint for Self-Improving Agents](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-11-self-improving-agents.md): Karpathy's autoresearch reveals the structure that makes self-improvement work: three separations (judge, subject, process), a keep/revert loop, and well-chosen fixed points.
- [Loom: The Case for Building on Opal](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-loom.md): An existing Elixir port of the Pi coding agent already solves the hardest architectural problem. One architect, frontier models, five weeks.
- [Porting OpenClaw's Core to Elixir](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-porting-openclaw-to-elixir.md): A practical assessment of porting the core gateway and agent loop to Elixir, keeping the TypeScript channels, and bridging them.
- [Runtime Self-Modification: Beyond TypeScript](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-runtime-self-modification.md): Why building a self-modifying agent in TypeScript is writing letters to your future self. Common Lisp offers depth, Elixir/BEAM offers safety.
- [Making OpenClaw Self-Aware: How Can an AI Agent Patch Its Own Code?](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-openclaw-self-modification.md): Five approaches to giving an AI agent a coherent mental model of its own codebase.
- [What if the LLM Thought in S-Expressions?](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-02-13-sexp-native-editing.md): Designing an AST-native code editing protocol for LLMs using Lisp's homoiconicity.
- [Lunar Habitat Life Support Systems](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-02-13-lunar-life-support-systems.md): Companies, technologies, and supply chains for atmospheric control and CO₂ scrubbing in next-generation lunar stations.

## Optional

- [Full content of all posts](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/llms-full.txt)
